architecture.summary: This is a survey paper that synthesizes literature on self-feedback mechanisms in large language models. It proposes a unified theoretical framework called Self-Feedback, consisting of two modules: Self-Evaluation (which captures internal consistency signals from model expressions at response, decoding, or latent layers through sampling methodologies) and Self-Update (which leverages those signals to enhance either the model's response or the model parameters themselves). The paper frames internal consistency as sampling-based consistency across different model layers and classifies methods by task and line of work rather than by architectural innovation. No novel model architecture is introduced; instead, the paper provides a meta-level taxonomy of existing approaches.

biblio.title: Internal Consistency and Self-Feedback in Large Language Models: A Survey

claims.statement: Large language models often exhibit deficient reasoning or generate hallucinations, and internal consistency—the consistency in expressions among LLMs' latent, decoding, or response layers based on sampling methodologies—is central to these core challenges.
On the reasoning benchmark GSM8K, the Self-Consistency method which uses majority voting increased answer accuracy by about 17.9%.
The Self-Contradict strategy, which generates different samples to identify self-contradictory content, showed that even GPT-4 was able to induce self-contradictions at rates of 15.7%.
Models exhibit low consistency in refusing 'I Don't Know' (IDK) questions, indicating they lack Self-Knowledge—the ability to express what they know and do not know.

The paper proposes the 'Hourglass Evolution of Internal Consistency' hypothesis: model answer consistency follows an hourglass pattern starting from lower latent layers (random judgments), through intermediate decoding layers (high certainty), to higher response layers (greater variability), suggesting that output tokens can interfere with and collapse latent reasoning.

The Self-Feedback framework formalizes internal consistency mining as a two-stage process: Self-Evaluate (obtaining feedback signal from sampling set) followed by Self-Update (using feedback to either update expressions or model parameters), with extensions to updating external models or students models.

The paper proposes 'Consistency Is (Almost) Correctness' hypothesis, linking internal consistency improvements to reasoning elevation and hallucination alleviation across response, decoding, and latent layers.

The paper proposes 'The Paradox of Latent and Explicit Reasoning': while models possess latent reasoning abilities, these are weak, and enhancing intermediate entity signals does not significantly improve model responses, suggesting current LLM architectures struggle with robust latent reasoning.
